summary:
Zhang Qian's Western Missions (138–114 BC) were diplomatic expeditions sent by [[event:1127|Emperor Wu of Han]] into Central Asia to seek allies against the [[event:1133|Xiongnu]], which, though they failed in their immediate military purpose, opened China's knowledge of and contact with the western world and established the routes that became the [[event:1129|Silk Road]].

Zhang Qian was captured by the Xiongnu on his first mission and held for over a decade before escaping and continuing westward to Bactria and Sogdia. He returned with detailed accounts of the [[event:1173|Greco-Bactrian Kingdom]]'s successor states, the kingdoms of Ferghana (with their prized 'heavenly horses'), Parthia, and even vague knowledge of Rome and India. His reports transformed Chinese geographical understanding of the world and revealed that products could be exchanged with distant civilizations. Emperor Wu launched a second mission and then sustained diplomatic contact with Central Asian kingdoms, primarily to acquire Ferghana horses superior to the steppe ponies used by the Xiongnu. The commerce that developed around these diplomatic missions became the Silk Road — a network of exchange that would transform Eurasian civilization for centuries.
